Ontogeny of the spinal cord dorsal horn

Highlights:

- Temporal cohorts of late-born dorsal neurons give rise to neuronal families
- SequenJally-born excitatory neuron families form adjacent laminae
- Laminar structure specifically requires excitatory (but not inhibitory or sensory) neurons
- Graded expression of Zic transcripJon factors directs fine neuronal idenJty

Summary :

The dorsal horn of the mammalian spinal cord is an exquisite example of form serving funcJon. It is comprised of diverse neuronal populaJons stacked into laminae, each of which receives different circuit connecJons and plays specialized roles in behavior. An outstanding quesJon is how this organizaJon emerges during development from an apparently homogeneous pool of neural progenitors. Here, we found that dorsal neurons are diversified by Jme, with families of related cell types born as temporal cohorts, and by a spaJal-molecular gradient that specifies the full array of individual cell types. Excitatory dorsal neurons then se'le in a chronotopic arrangement that transforms their progressive birthdates into anatomical order. This establishes the dorsal horn laminae, as these neurons are also required for spaJal organizaJon of inhibitory neurons and sensory axons. This work reveals essenJal ontogeneJc principles that shape dorsal progenitors into the diverse cell types and architecture that subserve sensorimotor behavior.

Keywords:

Spinal cord, dorsal horn, ontogeny, development, interneurons, cell types, lamina, structure, temporal specificaJon, Zic

Introduc;on

The dorsal horn of the spinal cord is crucial for some of the most important neural tasks of vertebrate animals, from processing pain and touch signals from the body to implemenJng sensorimotor control. It relies on a highly structured organizaJon in which dozens of molecularly discrete excitatory and inhibitory neuronal cell types are arrayed within anatomical layers, or laminae 1-4 . Each cell type has a characterisJc transcripJonal profile and 'families' of closely related cell types are highly conserved across mammalian species 5-7 . Each cell type also has a characterisJc spaJal distribuJon that is a strong predictor of its synapJc inputs and stereotyped laminar zones 1,7-11 . For example, neurons in lamina I and II process pain, itch, and temperature 8,12-17 , those in lamina III and IV process light touch 8,10,18-21 , and those in lamina V and VI process the sense of body-posiJon and contribute to motor control 9,22-31 . Overall, the that supports normal behavioral funcJon.

Here, we examined mouse dorsal horn development from neurogenesis unJl the establishment and mouse mutant analysis. We found that the families of the mature dorsal horn neuron types emerged as progressive waves of dIL neurons during late spinal neurogenesis. The excitatory families then se'led into a chronotopic order, with progressively born families se'ling into adjacent lamina. To test the hypothesis that dorsal excitatory neurons establish the laminar structure of the dorsal horn, we geneJcally eliminated dorsal excitatory, dorsal inhibitory, or sensory neurons and found that dorsal excitatory neurons specifically were crucial for the proper anatomical organizaJon of the dorsal horn. We also discovered a spaJal gradient of Zic transcripJon factors in dorsal progenitors and post-mitoJc dIL neurons that sub-divided each

funcJon, as the sensory and descending pathways that target the dorsal horn each terminate in arrangement of diverse cell types in laminae is a hallmark of the dorsal horn and the framework Despite our detailed knowledge of these cell types and structure, there is an enduring mystery in our understanding of the dorsal horn of the spinal cord: we sJll do not know how all this diversity and anatomical organizaJon comes to be. This is in sharp contrast with development of the ventral horn - a textbook example of developmental logic 32,33 . The ventral horn is mainly formed during an early phase of spinal neurogenesis, when secreted ligands spaJally pa'ern the progenitors into eleven discrete lineage domains that each give rise to a 'cardinal class' of interneurons 34-43 . Then, as neurogenesis proceeds, something surprising happens among a small group of late dorsal progenitors: they lose their early lineage restricJon, expand dramaJcally, and form a large common pool of pdL progenitors (progenitors of dorsal late) that generate the inhibitory (dILA; dorsal interneuron late) and excitatory (dILB) neurons that comprise the dorsal spinal cord 35,36,44-47 . How does this seemingly homogeneous populaJon of progenitors generate the highly diverse and highly structured cellular organizaJon of the dorsal horn? of laminar structure using neuronal birthdaJng, transcripJonal profiling, spaJal transcriptomics, family into more refined cell types, generaJng an impressive array of approximately 150 molecularly discrete types. Together, this work uncovered the underlying developmental mechanisms that transform neural progenitors into the dorsal horn of the spinal cord and thereby establish the neural substrates for sensorimotor control.

Results

A transcrip+onal atlas of the late embryonic dorsal horn

Our understanding of cell type organizaJon and development has exploded recently due to advances in single cell profiling that enable powerful new descripJve studies. We and others have applied these methods to study spinal cord biology in many different contexts, but there is a persistent gap in our knowledge: the crucial period in which dorsal spinal neurons differenJate from relaJvely generic immature neurons into the diverse cell types of the dorsal horn. Describing this period in detail should therefore provide insights into the processes of neuronal diversificaJon, how cell types relate to each other, and laminar development.

To examine the full complement of dorsal horn neurons, we needed to define the earliest period ajer which all neurons have been born; accordingly, we charted a detailed Jmecourse of spinal neurogenesis. EdU, which is incorporated into the DNA of dividing cells, was injected into female mice with Jmed pregnancies at half-day Jmepoints from embryonic day (E) E10.5 through E13.5, covering the general period of spinal cord neurogenesis. At E16.5, when the dorsal horn's laminar structure is evident 48 , we analyzed the distribuJon of EdU+ neurons, including both dorsal inhibitory neurons (marked by Pax2) and dorsal excitatory neurons (marked by Lmx1b) (Figure 1A). Nearly all neurons that se'led in the dorsal horn were born between E11.0 and E13.0 (Figure 1B), and neurogenesis was essenJally complete by E13.5, consistent with previous work 36,37,49-51 . Based on these results, we generated a single-cell RNAsequencing dataset of spinal cords at E14.5 and E16.5 (Figure S1A). We profiled 195,146 spinal cord cells (Figure S1, Tables S1 and S2) and obtained 94,048 neurons (Figure 1C). Hierarchical clustering was used to reveal mulJple levels of spinal neuron organizaJon. At the highest level, neurons were divided into three major groups that each accounted for approximately one third of the dataset.

Group 1 was comprised of the cardinal spinal cord neuron types including dI1, dI2, dI3, dI4, dI5, dI6, v0c, v0d, v0v, v1, v2a, v2b, and v3 interneurons, motoneurons, as well as Hb9 interneurons and cerebrospinal fluid contacJng neurons. Semi-supervised clustering yielded the full set of expected spinal lineage classes, which we idenJfied by expression of classic markers (Figure 1C,D, Tables S3 and S4) and by correlaJon with a well-annotated atlas of the period of spinal neurogenesis (E9.5 - E13.5) by Delile and colleagues (see Methods) 42,52,53 .

Groups 2 and 3 represented the putaJve dILA neurons (Group 2) and dILB neurons (Group 3), based on the expression of general embryonic dorsal markers (shared with putaJve dI4 and dI5 classes), modest residual expression of early post-mitoJc markers (suggesJng recent neurogenesis), later-born temporal transcripJon factors, as well as the expression of known markers of mature dorsal neurons, the region where dILA/B neurons reside 7,35,36,52 (Figure 1C,D). Given that late-born dILA and dILB neurons share some gene expression with their early-born dI4 and dI5 neurons counterparts, we sought disJnguishing characterisJcs between them to jusJfy a point of demarcaJon in our dataset. Exploratory analysis revealed that the transcripJon factor Zic1 was expressed in putaJve dILA and dILB neurons, but not dI4 and dI5 neurons. Zic1 is expressed in the dorsal progenitor zone throughout neurogenesis and also appears in postmitoJc neurons around E11.5, which aligns with the emergence of the first dIL neurons 54,55 . Immunofluorescence confirmed this pa'ern and showed that Zic1 protein expression in neurons was coincident with the dramaJc dorso-ventral expansion of the Lbx1-producing progenitors at the onset of dIL neuron producJon (Figure S2). In transcriptomic data, Zic1 expression was highly enriched in Group 2 and Group 3 relaJve to dI4 and dI5 clusters and similarly divided early and late 'dI4' and 'dI5' neurons in a single cell atlas from the period of acJve spinal neurogenesis 52 (Figure S2). Accordingly, we used Zic1 expression to delineate the boundaries between dI4-dILA and dI5-dILB (see Methods).

The internal organizaJon of dILA and dILB neurons has not been analyzed in depth before, limiJng our understanding of how dIL neurons diversify into the dozens of cell types in the mature dorsal horn. Sub-clustering of dILA and dILB yielded six classes within each Group that we designated dILA1, dILA2, dILA3, dILA4, dILA5, dILA6, dILB1, dILB2, dILB3, dILB4, dILB5, and dILB6 (Figure 1C,D and Figure S3,). Each of these classes were robust and well-separated, with high silhoue'e values, very low LISI scores, and high discriminaJon accuracy and precision with a random forest classifier (Figure S3-4, Tables S3 and S4). Comparison of these dILA and dILB classes with a harmonized transcriptomic atlas of postnatal spinal neurons 7 revealed that each dILA or dILB class correlated strongly with a single 'family' or a small group of related families (Figure 1E, Table S5), with the excepJon of dILA6 which did not have a clear postnatal counterpart. Thus, at E14.5 and E16.5, young dILA and dILB neurons were already well organized as families, the intermediate level of cell type hierarchy.

To examine the most refined level of cell type organizaJon hierarchy, that of specific cell types, we sub-clustered each family and found approximately 150 molecularly disJnguishable clusters (Figure 1F and Figure S3-5, Tables S6-S8). To determine whether refined dIL neuron types were segmentally restricted, we compared the cell type proporJons in samples derived from cervical, thoracic, and lumbar segments. We found that refined dIL neuron-types were largely present at all segments, with a few modest differences (Figure S3).

This work provided the first comprehensive characterizaJon of embryonic spinal neurons postneurogenesis, allowing us to link the transcriptomic profiles of progenitor lineage domains with their differenJated progeny. In parJcular, we found that both the family and refined type organizaJon of the mature dorsal horn are already present shortly ajer neurogenesis.

Temporal diversifica+on of embryonic dILA and dILB families

What processes diversify pdL progenitors into the dILA/B families? During the early wave of spinal neurogenesis, morphogen-directed spaJal pa'erning is the dominant driver that creates the set of canonical lineage classes 56-59 . However, spaJally restricted domains within the pdL progenitor pool have not been found, raising the possibility that a disJnct logic governs the development of the late-born dorsal neurons. Based on analogy with other laminated neural structures including the vertebrate reJna, corJcal plate, and tectum, we hypothesized that temporal pa'erning may govern the cell type diversificaJon and anatomical layering of the dorsal horn 60-64 .

We first noted that dILA and dILB families were organized as streams in a UMAP plot, with one end closest to early-born Zsx3+ dI4 and dI5 counterparts 50 and the other end closest to Robo3+ immature/migraJng neurons 65-69 and Sox2+ progenitors 70 (Figure 2A), suggesJve of conJnuous temporal variaJon. A complementary dimensionality reducJon and visualizaJon approach, PHATE, revealed a similar pa'ern (Figure 2B) 71 . Such inter-family relaJonships were further supported by the observaJon that the rare mistakes made by a classifier trained on family idenJty were between families that were adjacent in UMAP and PHATE plots (Figure S5). These exploratory findings prompted us to test whether families are organized temporally, which we did using three independent approaches.

First, we assessed the expression of the temporal transcripJon factors Onecut, Zsx, Nfi, and Neurod genes in each dILA and dILB family (Figure 2C). The Onecut genes, that ojen mark the earliest-born cell types, were not present in the dIL families, but we observed a smooth progression of Zsx (mid early-born), Neurod (mid late-born), and Nfi (latest-born) genes from dILA1/B1 through dILA6/B6. Therefore, both the inhibitory and excitatory families of the dorsal horn vary by a combinatorial code of temporal transcripJon factors.

Second, we reasoned that molecularly disJnct dILA and dILB families should appear in a progressive order if they are born successively. Recent work from Qiu et al. provided a large embryonic single cell atlas with high temporal resoluJon, with embryos staged and sorted based on refined anatomical criteria into quarter-day Jmepoints 72 . We extracted spinal cord neurons from this dataset, used label transfer to assign family idenJty, and then analyzed family composiJon over Jme. Amongst dILA families, the main order was dILA1 > dILA2 > dILA3 > dIL4 and dILA5, while dILA6 was detected throughout (Figure 2D). Amongst dILB families, the main order was dILB1 > dILB2 > dILB3 > dILB5 > dILB4, while dILB6 was detected throughout (Figure 2D). Third, we assessed dILA and dILB birthdates using Jmed EdU administraJon over half-day intervals combined with expression of family marker genes. This method measures birthdate more directly, though it provides slightly lower temporal resoluJon due to imprecision in Jming of concepJon relaJve to injecJon Jme and experimental analysis 73,74 . Amongst dILA families, we performed targeted analysis of three families for which either a single marker or a combinaJon of two markers was sufficient to idenJfy consJtuent neurons unambiguously in Jssue secJons (Figure 2E). dILA2, dILA4, and dILA5 were born in overlapping waves of neurogenesis with dILA2 born at E11.0-11.5, and dILA4 and dILA5 born around E11.5-12.0 (Figure 2F and Figure S6), with dILA4 preceding dILA5 slightly. Amongst dILB families, EdU birthdaJng was performed for all six families, or subsets thereof (Figure 2G). We found that the main birthdates for each group were as follows: dILB1 at E11.0-11.5, dILB2 at E11.5, dILB3 and dILB4 at E11.5-12.0, dILB5 at E12.0-12.5, and dILB6 at E12.0-13.5 (Figure 2H, Figure S7,8). These data showed an orderly progression of family birthdate.

From this, we conclude that families of dorsal horn neurons are born progressively across the period of late neurogenesis, and that temporal diversificaJon is a major organizing principle of the dorsal horn.

A chronotopic map of dILB families establishes the laminar structure of the dorsal horn

A common theme of neural development, parJcularly in laminated structures, is chronotopy: an organizaJonal principle in which progressively born neuronal subsets se'le into ordered anatomical arrangements, providing the underlying structure for mature circuit funcJon 6064,75,76 . To explore whether successively born dILA or dILB neurons could account for the laminar structure of the dorsal horn, we examined the anatomical locaJon of all dorsal inhibitory (Pax2+) and dorsal excitatory (Lmx1b+) neurons with respect to neuronal birthdate (EdU+). We found that dILA neurons born at parJcular Jmes had stereotypical spaJal distribuJons, but ojen spanned many laminae and had no spaJally congruent pa'ern across Jmepoints (Figure 3A,B). In contrast, we observed that progressively born Lmx1b+ neurons se'led into a coherent order: putaJve dI5 neurons, born at E10.5 35,36,77 , were found in lateral lamina V, followed by dILB neurons born at E11.0/E11.5 in lamina III/IV, those born at E12.0/E12.5 in lamina I/II, and those born at E13.0/E13.5 in part of lamina IIi/III and medial lamina V (Figure 3A,B).

We next assessed the spaJal distribuJon of each dILA/B family to determine whether the parJcular families followed the pa'ern of the overall Pax2+ and Lmx1b+ neurons. Xenium spaJal transcriptomics was used to profile gene expression in Jssue secJons from E16.5 mouse spinal cords (n=3) and predicted cell idenJJes 78 were mapped onto normalized cell coordinates from all replicates. As predicted by the EdU birthdaJng, we found that dILA families se'led into stereotypical locaJons that lacked an overarching temporal-spaJal order, while progressively born dILB families were found in progressively adjacent laminae (Figure 3C, S9). The highest density of the first-born dILB1 family was found in lamina IV-V, dILB2 was in lamina III-IV, dILB3 was in lamina II-III, and dILB4 was in lamina IIo. The progression then reversed direcJon, with dILB5 se'ling broadly throughout lamina II-III and dILB6 in medial lamina V as well as a streak along dorsal lamina V. The arrangement of dILA and dILB families corresponded to the progression of Pax2 and Lmx1b-birthdated neurons across neurogenesis.

The chronotopic development of dILB families raised the possibility that excitatory neuron organizaJon is the criJcal determinant of laminar structure in the dorsal horn. To test this hypothesis, we used mouse geneJcs to selecJvely remove each of the major classes of neurons from the dorsal horn: dorsal inhibitory neurons, dorsal excitatory neurons, and sensory neurons whose cell bodies are in the dorsal root ganglion but whose axons innervate the dorsal horn. We then compared the impact of each perturbaJon on the distribuJon of the remaining neural elements.

To remove dorsal inhibitory neurons from the spinal cord, we used Py1a Cre/Cre homozygous null embryos, in which the dI4/dILA neurons are trans-fated to become dI5/dILB neurons 46,79 . As expected, Pax2 expression was nearly absent from the dorsal horn, but the overall morphology of the Jssue appeared normal (Figure 4A). In situ hybridizaJon and immunofluoresence for markers of dILB and sensory neuron types revealed that their typical spaJal distribuJons were preserved (Figure 4A,C and Figure S10). SpaJal transcriptomics performed on Py1a Cre/Cre Jssue also revealed that predicted cell type locaJons for dILB families were normal (Figure 4E and Figure S11-12). Thus, inhibitory neurons were not required for the anatomical organizaJon of the dorsal horn.

To remove dorsal excitatory neurons from the spinal cord, we used Gsx1 -/-; Gsx2 -/-double mutant embryos, in which dI5/dILB neurons are trans-fated to become dI4/dILA neurons 45,80,81 . In Gsx double mutants, Lmx1b was nearly absent from the dorsal horn at E16.5, as expected, and the remaining Jssue displayed abnormal morphology (Figure 4B).

There was a striking spaJal disorganizaJon of the remaining neural components of the dorsal horn. The restricted laminar distribuJons of dILA neuron types found in wild-type embryos were lost  in  double  mutants,  with  some  neuron  types  present  in  a  mildly  expanded  range (Uts2b+/Frzb+ dILA2-H neurons) and others being sca'ered from the pia all the way to the base of the dorsal horn (Osr1+ dILA2-J neurons and Kcnip2+ dILA4 neurons) (Figure 4B,D,F and Figure S13-14). Despite this disorganizaJon, dILA types desJned for the same lamina were ojen found together in clumps, which may reflect cell-type intrinsic sorJng mechanisms. Sensory fibers were similarly disorganized, with PV+ large proprioceptor fibers crossing through the superficial dorsal horn (Figure 4B,D). We also observed neurons sca'ered amongst the dense longitudinal sensory fibers just below the pia, reflecJng an abnormal boundary between the gray and white ma'er (Figure 4B).

We also considered whether laminar organizaJon could be imposed on dIL neurons by an extrinsic source. At E16.5, the only other neural element in the dorsal horn are sensory fibers, as descending axons from the brain have not yet penetrated the dorsal gray ma'er 82-90 . To ablate sensory neurons, we used Pax3 Cre ;Isl2 lsl-DTA embryos 91,92 , which express diphtheria-toxin in sensory neurons (and in some other neural crest-derivaJves). As expected, this ablaJon led to loss of dorsal root ganglia and of sensory axons in the gray ma'er (Figure S15). In addiJon, Lissauer's tract and the dorsal columns were reduced dramaJcally in size and the dorsal horns ojen fused at the midline. Despite these changes, dILA and dILB neuron markers were present and displayed laminar pa'erns in the same dorso-ventral order as control spinal cords (Figure S15) consistent with a prior report 93 , indicaJng that sensory neuron innervaJon was not required for laminaJon.

Together, these data suggest a hierarchy of structure in the dorsal horn, in which excitatory neurons form laminar structures organized by birthdate and that inhibitory and sensory axons are dependent on this process to achieve their normal laminar distribuJons.

Spa+al-molecular diversifica+on of dIL neuron families generates refined cell types

The temporal diversificaJon process that generated the six dILA and six dILB families is unlikely explain the impressive molecular diversity of the ~150 refined dorsal spinal neuron types, so we next asked what other sources of variaJon may instruct dorsal neuron idenJty. To nominate candidate factors, we explored the most highly weighted genes for the top principal components explaining molecular variaJon within each dIL family (Table S10). This revealed that Zic transcripJon factors contributed highly to variaJon in nearly all families. In addiJon to Zic1, which we showed is expressed in post-mitoJc dIL neurons (Figure S2), there are four other mammalian members of the Zic zinc finger transcripJon factor family, Zic2, Zic3, Zic4, and Zic5, all of which have been shown to be present in the embryonic spinal dorsal horn, parJcularly within the progenitor domain 54,55,94-96 . Zics can interact with the transcripJon factors that are downstream of Shh, TGF-beta, and Wnt signaling - three major pathways that pa'ern the embryonic spinal cord 97-102 . This led us to hypothesize that Zic factors may regulate heterogeneity amongst pdL progenitors and the idenJty of refined neuron types in the dorsal spinal cord.

We first assessed whether Zic gene expression in dILA and dILB post-mitoJc neurons was related to the idenJty of refined neuron types. All five Zic genes were detected in UMAP feature plots amongst dILA and dILB neurons (Figure 5A) and we observed corresponding expression of all five Zic factors in the dorsal spinal cord of E16.5 embryonic Jssue secJons (Figure S16). Within the single cell data from all dILA families and most dILB families, Zic genes were expressed in a gradient, with refined cell types varying in both how many Zic genes were present and the expression levels of each individual Zic gene (Figure 5B). Zic1 showed the most widespread expression, followed by Zic4, Zic2, and then Zic5. Zic3 was only found in restricted cell types. Focusing on one family, dILA2, the refined types were present along a trajectory in UMAP space that varied smoothly in Zic expression levels from dILA2-M Zic High neurons through dILA2-X Zic Neg neurons (Figure 5C). Ranking dILA2 refined types by their Zic expression revealed that, while each type expressed unique markers, there were also shared markers amongst types that were either Zic High (eg. Pax5), Zic Med (eg. Rorb), or Zic Low/Neg (eg. Ma|) (Figure 5D). Overall, this analysis revealed that a gradient of Zic gene expression sub-divided most dIL families into refined types of dorsal spinal post-mitoJc neurons.

Cell fate restricJon and diversificaJon are typically established at the progenitor stage, but pdL progenitors have not been reported to display molecular variaJon. Since Zic expression has been reported previously in the dorsal spinal progenitor domain, we analyzed whether Zic expression varied in this context as well. Indeed, we found that a smooth gradient of Zic expression was present in single cell transcriptomic data from both E14.5 - E16.5 neurons as well as E11.5 - E12.5 progenitors, with Zic1 and Zic4 being the most broadly expressed and Zic2 and Zic5 being the most restricted (Figure 5E).

In embryonic spinal Jssue, Zic genes were present in a striking dorsal-ventral spaJal gradient in the E11.5-E12.5 progenitor domain, with highest expression in the dorsal-most part of progenitor domain and decreasing expression towards the ventral spinal cord. Zic1 extended the most ventrally, followed by Zic4, Zic2, and then Zic5 which displayed the most dorsally restricted expression; Zic3 had a disJnct and more restricted pa'ern (Figure 5F,G). The dorsalventral gradient of Zic1 protein expression was observed in both pdL progenitors and in postmitoJc Lbx1-expressing dILA/B neurons (Figure 5G). Thus, Zic factors were expressed in a molecular-spaJal gradient within pdL progenitors and their post-mitoJc progeny.

Zic expression could simply correlate with dorsal neuron diversity or it could be an important driver of the cell type repertoire. To test whether Zic factors play an instrucJve role in dorsal neuron diversificaJon, we examined Zic3 mutant embryos that have lost a spaJally restricted Zic 103,104 and Zic1 / Zic4 double mutant embryos (DKO) that have lost the most widely expressed Zic genes 105 .

Unlike the other Zic genes, Zic3 was detected in a faint bipolar gradient midway through the gradient of Zic1 (Figure 5E,F). MutaJon of Zic3 through the premature stop codon of the 'katun' mutant resulted in a mild increase in Zic Med refined types within several families (Figure S17). This suggests that Zic3 may act mainly as a transcripJonal repressor to modify the repertoire of refined dIL neuron types.

We hypothesized that the loss of both Zic1 and Zic4 would have a more profound impact on dorsal neuron development, shijing the domain of Zic-expressing progenitors and neurons dorsally and expanding the domain of Zic Neg progenitors and neurons. To test this predicJon, we analyzed the expression of Satb2 in spinal cords from Zic1/4 DKO embryos and control li'ermates. Satb2 was a marker of a refined neuron type (dILA2-X) with expression that iniJated in the transiJon zone near progenitors, thereby revealing the domain of origin of these newborn neurons around E12.5. As predicted, the domain of Satb2 -expressing dILA2-X neurons was expanded significantly and shijed dorsally (Figure 5H,I). We next used spaJal transcriptomics to assess the cell type composiJon of the dorsal horn in Zic1/4 DKO embryos and focused our analysis on dILA2 refined types. The Zic Neg dILA2-X type was significantly expanded and Zic Low types showed modest increases as well. Conversely, nearly all of the Zic Med and Zic High dILA2 types were significantly decreased upon loss of Zic1/4 (Figure 5J,K and Figure S17-19). Similar results were observed in other dIL families (Figure S18). Thus, the Zic molecular-spaJal gradient, anchored by the broadly expressed Zic1 and Zic4 genes, was essenJal for the proper development of refined dIL neuron types.

Discussion

We sought to uncover the general principles that govern the development of the spinal cord dorsal horn in mice, focusing on how its impressive cellular diversity and layered architecture emerge from a seemingly homogeneous pool of neural progenitors. We found that dorsal spinal progenitors give rise to mulJple generaJons of neurons that varied transcripJonally based on the Jme and spaJal posiJon from which they originated. Specifically, each successive wave of newborn neurons gave rise to an excitatory or inhibitory coarse 'family' of related neurons and each family was further divided into refined types based on a dorsal-ventral gradient of Zic transcripJon factors. Following neurogenesis, the young excitatory neurons se'led into an orderly chronotopic map and we found that this organizaJon was essenJal to the formaJon of the laminae of the dorsal horn. Altogether, we charted the detailed embryonic origins of mature dorsal neurons, discovered the developmental mechanisms that shape their molecular diversity, and revealed how their anatomical organizaJon is established, thereby laying down the neural substrates for sensorimotor control of behavior.

How is the conJnuous flow of developmental Jme converted into the various dILA and dILB families? We propose that the conserved dorsal cell type families reflect the staJsJcal modes of cell types generated over Jme, rather than discrete types separated by abrupt transiJons. This model is supported by the overlapping emergence of dILA and dILB families in the precisely Jmed single cell data; the superimposed waves of peak birthdates that we observed for each family in neuronal birthdaJng analysis; and the small amount of 'blurring' between temporally adjacent families. In this way, dorsal horn development may be similar to the formaJon of the mammalian reJna, in which the generaJon of cell types is determined by the stochasJc combinaJon of progressive changes in a transcripJon factor code and the parJcular Jming of progenitor cell cycle exit 106-109 . However, to determine the progenitor mechanisms of temporal cell type diversificaJon in the spinal cord, clonal analysis and/or new sequencing-based methods for tracking cell fate relaJonships over Jme will be needed.

Not only does developmental Jme guide the emergence of dorsal horn families, but we found that it is etched onto the structure of the mature spinal cord. A general theme of laminated structures in the nervous system is the presence of an anatomically organized microcircuit with precise afferent and efferent connecJons in each layer 110-113 . This is certainly the case for the adult spinal dorsal horn, parJcularly for sensory and descending brain axons that target specific lamina to cooperaJvely mediate sensorimotor control. Our data suggest that the chronotopic development of the dILB neurons establishes the spaJal landscape to receive these inputs at their proper locaJons. Progressively born excitatory dILB neurons se'led into neighboring layers and their loss profoundly disrupted the spaJal organizaJon of remaining neural elements including inhibitory neurons and sensory afferents. In contrast, loss of inhibitory dILA neurons or sensory neurons preserved normal laminar organizaJon, while descending axons from the brain only target spinal neurons ajer the lamina have already been established. An autonomous role for dorsal spinal neurons in self-organizing into their characterisJc layers is further supported by the intrinsic ability of cultured dorsal interneurons to form laminae upon transplantaJon into spinal Jssue, with sensory and corJcospinal axons invading these miniature dorsal structures in a spaJally appropriate manner 114 . Together, these findings suggest that excitatory dorsal neurons form a laminar map which is imparted onto inhibitory dorsal neurons, sensory neurons, and likely also descending pathways from the brain.

UnJl now, late dorsal progenitors were thought to be a homogeneous group, raising quesJons about how the impressive diversity of neuron types is generated. We found a dorsal-to-ventral gradient of Zic transcripJon factors that pa'erned pdL progenitors, created an array of variants within dIL families, and was required to generate the normal complement of dorsal neurons. Zic genes had previously been described in the dorsal spinal cord, where Zic1 regulates early progenitor proliferaJon 55 and Zic2 is known to mark mature spinal neurons that contribute to the post-synapJc dorsal column pathway 19 . It is not known how Zic proteins regulate dorsal neuron diversity, but an interesJng hint may come from their similarity to Gli transcripJon factors 97,115,116 . As Gli factors mediate Hedgehog signaling to pa'ern the spinal cord ventral horn, Gli and Zic proteins may work together to establish countervailing molecular gradients. Future work should probe the molecular mechanisms that transform the Zic gradient into gene expression signatures that characterize dorsal horn neurons.

Overall, our work supports a model in which four major principles govern dorsal horn ontogeny (Figure 6). First, six temporal cohorts of dIL neurons are born successively and give rise to the families of dorsal neuronal types. Second, each temporal cohort is pa'erned along a dorsalventral axis by Zic transcripJon factors, thereby fracJonaJng each family into a range of variant types. Third, individual pdL progenitors can give rise to inhibitory dILA or excitatory dILB neurons as directed by Notch signaling, a process we did not study here but which has been shown previously 45,47 . Fourth, the excitatory neurons establish the anatomical structure of the dorsal horn by se'ling into an ordered distribuJon based on their birthdate.

As this picture comes into focus, the similarity to drosophila opJc lobe development is striking: there is a similar number of discrete transcripJonal cell types, similar temporal diversificaJon which is transformed into a spaJal arrangement for precise circuits, similar dorso-ventral spaJal pa'erning that is most likely coordinated by the same signaling pathways (Shh/Hh, BMP/Dpp, and Wnt/Wg), similar binary cell fate decisions controlled by the Notch pathway, and even a possible similar role for Zic/Opa factors in regulaJng neuronal diversity 117-121 . These shared features in evoluJonarily distant Jssues and organisms may be coincidence, or they may reflect convergent forces that produce sensory systems specialized for mulJ-modal processing. In parJcular, the high degree of neuronal diversity (generated by the three-fold combinaJon of temporal, spaJal, and Notch mechanisms) and the layered anatomical microcircuit may facilitate the parsing, sharpening, blending, integraJon, and feature extracJon from different neural informaJon streams. The next fronJer belongs to contextualizing this cellular diversity and structure in terms of behavior - that is, to reconcile these neuronal elements with their contribuJons to sensaJon, autonomic control, and movement.

Embryonic spinal cord ;ssue:

Appropriate pairs of male and female mice were introduced to the same home cage at approximately 18:00, and checked daily thereajer each morning at approximately 10:00 for vaginal plugs. 12:00 on the day a plug was detected was considered (embryonic day) E0.5, and embryos were dissected at approximately noon on the embryonic day indicated by the experiments described here (below) and in Results. ParJcular care was taken to ensure EdU birthdaJng was as accurate as possible; for these experiments, males and females were paired only one night per week, and separated in the morning regardless of whether a plug was detected. Only females which plugged received a subsequent i.p injecJon of EdU (more details below), ensuring that Jme of concepJon was constrained to approximately the dark period (i.e. with an uncertainty of plus or minus 8 hours).

On the indicated embryonic day, female mice were administered CO2 euthanasia, and embryos were promptly dissected and washed in ice-cold 1X PBS (except in cold HBSS for preparaJon for single-cell suspensions in the 10X Chromium assay). The spinal column was severed at the spinal cord-hindbrain juncJon, and the column was further dissected from the torso. For experiments prepared at the NaJonal InsJtutes of Health, the vertebral body was further dissected to expose the ventral surface of the spinal cord to fixaJve; for experiments performed elsewhere, dissecJon only involved isolaJon of the column with contained spinal cord. Spinal columns were immersion-fixed in ice-cold 4% paraformaldehyde for either 4-6 hours for immunohistochemical applicaJons, or overnight (20-24 hours) for in-situ hybridizaJon applicaJons (both 10X Xenium and RNA Scope). Ajer fixaJon, Jssue samples were briefly rinsed in 1X PBS (<10 minutes) and then equilibrated to a soluJon of 30% sucrose plus 1X PBS overnight or unJl Jssue sunk to the bo'om of the vessel containing the Jssue. In all cases Jssues were agitated on an orbital or nutaJng shaker (depending on the vessel type) during the fixaJon and cryoprotecJon intervals in order to facilitate equilibraJon of Jssues to the PFA or sucrose soluJons.

Genotyping: Primer sets are detailed in the Key Resources table. Genotyping was done as described by original publicaJons for each allele (noted in Key Resources table). Expected band sizes are: Py1a Cre (Wild-type: 123 bp, Cre: 300 bp), Zic1/4 DKO (Wild-type: 465 bp, Mutant: 400 bp), Gsx1 +/-(Wild-type: 219 bp, Mutant: 180 bp), Gsx2 Ra (otherwise referred to as Gsx2 +/-, Wildtype: 298 bp, Mutant: 130 bp). Pax3 Cre and Isl2 DTA embryos were genotyped by Transnetyx (Cordova, TN, USA) using custom primer sets for the alleles (see Jackson Laboratory pages for original primer sets). For purposes of colony maintenance, Pax3 Cre mice were genotyped by the presence of a white splotch on their belly fur at the Jme of weaning (typical of mice bearing one null Pax3 allele). Zic3 Ka embryos were genotyped as sex-typed as follows: Genomic DNA was prepared for genotyping as previously described 104 ; 50 ng of genomic DNA was added to each genotyping reacJon. The Zic3 Ka strain was genotyped via Allelic DiscriminaJon using the oligonucleoJdes Ark241_Katun_F and Ark242_Katun_R and probes Wildtype: 5' 6FAM and G>T mutaJon: 5' VIC, with TaqMan® Universal PCR Master Mix (Life Technologies; Cat. No. 4304437). The products were amplified and analysed using a StepOnePlus™ Real-Time PCR System (Applied Biosystems®) and StepOne sojware (version 2.2.2; Applied Biosystems®). The embryos were sex-typed using MyTaq HS Red (Bioline: BIO-25048) and touchdown PCR with an iniJal annealing temperature of 60°C, as previously described 104 . The sex was determined using the oligonucleoJdes Ark1004_Smcx_y_F and Ark1005_Smcx_y_R. Amplicons were separated via agarose gel electrophoresis (2% agarose in 1X tris-borate-EDTA buffer).

Method Details:

Birthda;ng of spinal cord ;ssue: Pregnant female mice (detailed above) were injected with an intra-peritoneal soluJon containing 50 mg/kg of 5-Ethynyl-2ʹ-deoxyuridine (EdU, Thermo Fisher ScienJfic) at a concentraJon of 2 mg/ml in sterile 1x PBS. InjecJons were performed on separate pregnant females at either 12:00 (for the midpoint of an embryonic day - i.e. E12.5) or

0:00 (for the beginning of an embryonic day - i.e. E12.0). Only one injecJon at only one parJcular Jme point was performed for each individual female. Two separate cohorts of embryos were produced for the present Jmecourse - an iniJal cohort which were briefly fixed (4-6 h) suitable for immunohistochemistry (examined in figures 1 and 3), and a second cohort where equal numbers of embryos from each li'er were fixed for either 4-6 h or for 20-24 h (more suitable for in-situ hybridizaJon) and which were examined in figure 2. A cohort of embryos with IHC and ISH-suitable fix condiJons were necessary to make within-li'er comparisons of EdU co-labeling between all dIL families, some of which are best labeled with either IHC or ISH. Histochemistry to detect the presence of EdU in spinal cord nuclei is detailed below.

Cryosec;oning: All Jssues were frozen while immersed in OCT compound in a cubic mould. Samples from li'ermate embryos were always embedded and frozen in the same block of OCT. Moulds were immersed in 100% ethanol chilled with dry ice up to the top of the mould, though direct contact of OCT compound and ethanol was avoided. Blocks were maintained at -80 °C ajer freezing unJl cryosecJoning, at which points blocks were equilibrated to the cryostat temperature (generally -18 °C to -24 °C) and cut in 25 μm secJons (except for 10X Xenium, which were cut at 10 μm). SecJons were mounted on microscope slides which were then stored at -80 °C prior to histochemistry.

Immunohistochemistry (IHC) : All IHC was performed using published primary anJbodies (detailed in Key Resources table). A wide variety of secondary anJbodies were used here and were Donkey IgG Alexa-fluor conjugates purchased from either Invitrogen or (for Donkey anJGuinea pig secondary anJbodies) Jackson Immunolabs. Working diluJons of anJbodies from their manufactured concentraJons are detailed in the Key Resources table. Briefly, slides were removed from -80 °C and dried at 37 °C for at least 20 minutes. SecJons were then surrounded by a hydrophobic barrier (Vector Laboratories) and dried at room temperature another 5 minutes. SecJons were then washed three Jmes successively for 10 minutes each using cold 1x PBS at 4 °C. Working diluJons of primary anJbodies were prepared in 1x PBS with 0.1% Triton X100, applied inside the hydrophobic barrier, and incubated overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently slides were again washed three Jmes successively for 10 minutes each using cold 1x PBS at 4 °C. Working diluJons of appropriate Alexa-Fluor-conjugated secondary anJbodies together with DAPI (for nuclear staining) were prepared in 1x PBS with 0.1% Triton X-100, applied inside the hydrophobic barrier, and incubated for 1 hour at 4 °C. Finally, slides were once again washed three Jmes successively for 10 minutes each using cold 1x PBS at 4 °C. Ajer the final 1x PBS wash was removed from the slide, 2-4 drops of Prolong-Gold (Thermo Fisher ScienJfic) was applied to the slide and the coverslip was gently laid to spread the medium and avoid trapping bubbles. Covered slides were allowed to dry at least 1 hour prior to imaging and stored at 4 °C prior to and ajer imaging.

In-situ hybridiza;on (ISH): All ISH was performed based on the published protocols for ACD Bio RNA Scope MulJplex Fluoresence (version 2) assay. All reagents and probes were manufactured by ACD Bio and are listed in the Key Resources table. Target retrieval was omi'ed from the Jssue pretreatment protocol for all Jssues examined here, due to the step producing excessive damage to embryonic secJons. Probe fluorescence signal was not evidently changed in embryonic spinal cord secJons receiving versus not receiving target retrieval. Briefly, secJons were dried for two hours at room temperature prior to the modified Jssue pretreatment protocol. HybridizaJon, development of fluorescence signal, nuclear staining with DAPI, and cover-slip applicaJon was done exactly as described by protocol. Covered slides were allowed to dry at least 1 hour prior to imaging and stored at 4 °C prior to and ajer imaging. Fluorescence was developed using the newer TSA-Vivid dyes (Vivid 520, Vivid 570 and Vivid 650).

Pixel-frequency depth histograms: For selected IHC and ISH signals, histograms of pixelfrequency were gathered from E16.5 wild-type, P81a -null, Gsx1/2 -null and sensory neuronablated spinal cords. This was done using the Plot Profile funcJon of ImageJ/Fiji on 8-bit grey thresholded images. To measure pixels where two markers colocalize images of both markers were separately thresholded and pixels were mulJplied using ImageJ/Fiji's image calculator, with the histogram being applied to the product image. One to three secJons per biological replicate were analyzed and, whenever possible, each hemisecJon was measured separately. The histogram was collected verJcally, with one end of the range at the pial surface of the superficial dorsal horn midway through its mediolateral extent, and the other end of the range at the dorsal-ventral border of the ventricular midline (where both sides become adhered). The histogram width (i.e. how much sampling was done) varied, and generally encompassed one third of the total dorsal horn width except smaller when avoiding sampling autofluorescent vasculature. Histogram bins were compressed to a common number (80) in order to allow comparisons between genotypes, and bin frequencies were normalized to the total counts for that parJcular bin per replicate. Lamina VI was mostly excluded from this analysis due to the lack of dILB neurons there; accordingly, the dorsal-most 56 bins were plo'ed in figures, with color saturaJon corresponding to bin weights.

EdU histochemistry reac;on: In the event of EdU histochemistry, slides being reacted for IHC or ISH were not covered at the end of the protocol; instead, they were washed in 1x PBS with 0.1% Triton X-100 for 30 minutes at 4 °C. Following this, they were incubated at room temperature in a soluJon of EdU reacJon buffer, buffer addiJve, copper (II) sulfate, and Alexa-Fluor-conjugated azide (Alexa-Fluor-488 used in conjuncJon with Vivid-570 and Vivid-650 dyes on RNA Scopereacted slides, or Alexa-Fluor-647 with IHC-reacted slides having omi ng Alexa-Fluor-647conjugated secondary anJbodies) at the raJos indicated for the Click-iT EdU Cell ProliferaJon Kit (Thermo Fisher ScienJfic). They were subsequently washed three Jmes successively for 10 minutes each using cold 1x PBS at 4 °C, cover-slipped and stored per IHC protocols above.

Microscopy: All micrographs presented here were obtained either from inverted confocal microscopes (Zeiss LSM-800), or from a Zeiss AxioImager Z2 equipped with Apotome 3 structured illuminaJon and DAPI, GFP, DsRed, Cy5, and Cy7 filter cubes using Zeiss ZEN sojware. In either case, micrographs were composed of a Jle-scan of spinal cord secJons using a 20x objecJve as well as a z-stack of either 6-9 intervals centered approximately mid-way through the Jssue secJon's thickness. For experiments using Apotome, five images were taken per field of view, each using different posiJons of the structured illuminaJon grid, and were deconvoluted using ZEN's Apotome RAW Convert funcJon. Image arrays from both Apotome and confocal experiments were sJtched using Zeiss ZEN's sJtching funcJon; all channels at all zposiJons were sJtched with reference to nuclear DAPI signal from images in the mid-point of the stack. Images were stored in Zeiss' .czi format, and were subsequently loaded into Fiji (Image J, v2.3.0) with the current Bio-Formats plugin.

EdU counts: All birthdaJng data in this study was done on cervical spinal cord secJons, between C3-C8. Images with EdU and signal from two other markers (either from IHC or ISH) were counted in ImageJ using the Cell Counter plugin. Cells were considered posiJve for EdU if the nucleus was mostly filled with signal and excluded if the nucleus was a disconJnuous set of puncta. Overlap with either transcripJon factor IHC signal or with RNA Scope was considered as a posiJve colocalized cell and was counted separately to either cells only labeled with EdU or cells only labeled with cell-type markers respecJvely. Anchor points were labeled on each secJon in order to allow coordinate normalizaJon in data processing. Briefly, a set of ten points were added to each secJon: two on either dorsal horn's most dorsal extent; two on either dorsal horn's lateral-most extent, one at the base of the dorsal columns, two on either ventral horn's lateral-most extent, two on either ventral horn's most ventral extent, and one on a point along the midline where ependymal/progenitor cells diverge dorsally but are adhered ventrally; this point is considered to be the boundary between dorsal and ventral horns. Coordinates are then exported using the Measure tool.

EdU counts - cumula;ve counts sta;s;cs: At least one embryo was assessed per li'er; for each embryo, the fracJon of co-labeled neurons as a fracJon of all neurons of any dIL family at any parJcular Jmepoint of EdU pulse were represented as a fracJon. The mean of these fracJons across all embryos of a li'er was counted as a single data point, as we judged that variance across li'ers was higher than within li'ers, hence the insufficiency of only one li'er for this analysis (regardless of whether that li'er had embryos in triplicate).

To produce cumulaJve counts for each Jmepoint, the mean co-labeling of each dIL family for each Jmepoint was produced. This value was then normalized to the fracJon of mean colabeling across all Jmepoints, so that it reflected the percent of dIL family neurons born at a certain Jme point. From here, mean co-labeling for all prior Jmepoints were added to the replicate data of each Jmepoint such that within Jmepoint variaJon would be preserved but that mean cumulaJve co-labeling at the final Jmepoint would be 100%. Data were plo'ed as mean ± standard deviaJon and sigmoidal fit curves were calculated and plo'ed in Graphpad Prism using 'XY analyses' -> 'Interpolate a standard curve' -> 'Sigmoidal, 4PL, X is concentraJon'. Sigmoidal fit could not be done for two Jmepoints (dILA2 and dILB1) where substanJal neurogenesis between E10.5 and E11.0 produced a negligible slow-growth phase to the curve; these curves were instead calculated according to the 'Asymmetric Sigmoidal, 5PL, X is concentraJon' fit. EC50/IC50 values reported by the standard curve interpolaJon were plo'ed as verJcal lines on the Jme (x-) axis of standard curve plots in colors corresponding to each of six dILA or dILB families respecJvely.

Cell coordinate rota;on and normaliza;on: For EdU cell count coordinates (and later also for spaJal transcriptomics cell coordinates), cell coordinates were normalized so that staJsJcs and graphics could be produced systemaJcally on cell coordinates across secJons and experimental groups. To do this, anchor points (described in EdU counts above) were used to transform all coordinates idenJfied from a secJon. Two midline coordinate pairs (from the dorsal columns base and at the dorsalhorn-ventral-horn juncJon) were used to rotate all coordinates such that both coordinate pairs had idenJcal x-coordinates. Each hemisecJon was then stretched to fit an ideal diagram of a spinal cord; y-coordinates from the coordinate pairs at the dorsal surface of the dorsal horn and ventral surface of the ventral horn were normalized to either +2000 or 2000 arbitrary units of distance. Likewise, the x-coordinates of coordinate pairs at the lateralmost extent of each dorsal horn were normalized to either -2000 or +2000. X and Y coordinates of all intervening spinal cord neuron data were linearly transformed based on their distance from these anchor points. AddiJonally, neurons were linearly transformed based on their proximity to the y-coordinates of the lateral dorsal horn coordinate pairs or to the y-coordinate of the anchor at the base of the dorsal columns. The normalized value for these were also arbitrary, and changed based on segmental group to best fit an ideal spinal cord diagram; because of this, staJsJcal comparisons between data from different spinal cord segments were not done - only within segment and between EdU pulse Jme (or genotype). Cell coordinates from all condiJons were collated in a single database and analyzed further.

Contour plots: Due to variability in Jme of concepJon, a certain amount of overlap in terms of the pa'erns of EdU-dIL family co-labeling was expected and observed for adjacent families, and between group differences are difficult to judge on a sca'er plot. We therefore prepared and plo'ed 2-dimensional kernel density esJmates for EdU birthdaJng cell coordinate data. This was done using the kde2d funcJon from the R MASS library. Kernel density esJmates were produced at a resoluJon of 100 for cell coordinates from all li'er replicates and all EdU Jmepoints, which were then collated in a database. Contours were plo'ed in ggplot2 using geom_contour_filled and drawn to fit the densest 50%, 25%, 15%, 5% and 2.5% of points. Broader contour levels were drawn with greater transparency to represent lower cell density in these levels.

Cell prepara;on for single-cell RNA-sequencing: Female wild-type mice with a pregnancy dated to embryonic day E14.5 or E16.5 were used. Each experimental replicate consisted of a pooled li'er of 3-10 spinal cords taken from embryos of one single pregnant female.

Embryos were rapidly dissected and immersed in cold 1X PBS. Spinal cords were sequenJally dissected as described above and placed in cold dissecJon medium (50 ml HBSScalcium/magnesium-free with phenol red (Gibco) + 750 µl HEPES (1M, Gibco)). When possible, extra care was taken to remove meninges and dorsal root ganglia from the isolated cord. From here, appropriate spinal cord segments were dissected from the cord, and then cut into fine pieces with dissecJng spring scissors. Either cervical, thoracic or lumbosacral cords were isolated based on the experimental group. In either case, care was taken not to include transiJonal zones of the cord (e.g. where cervical tapers into thoracic or where thoracic begins to enlarge to lumbar); due to this, these regions are likely underrepresented in our data.

Pooled spinal cord Jssues were then added to a 15 ml conical tube containing dissociaJon medium (100 units Papain (Worthington), 4.75 ml HBSS+CM (calcium and magnesium; Gibco), and 250 µl of DNAse (Worthington, 1000 units resuspended in 500 µl of HBSS+CM)). The tube was gently inverted several Jmes every 5 minutes while leaving the tube to incubate at 37 °C between inversions. Ajer 20 minutes, the suspension was triturated gently with a series of three fire-polished glass pipe'es (Fisher ScienJfic) with progressively finer bores. Suspensions were then centrifuged for 8 minutes at 850 rpm; ajer this, the supernatant was removed and the cells were gently resuspended with a soluJon of 4.5 ml HBSS+CM, 600 µl ovomucoid protease inhibitor soluJon (Worthington, resuspended in 30 ml HBSS+CM), and 250 µl DNAse (as above). Debris was allowed to se'le for 10 minutes, at which point the supernatant with suspended cells was added to a second 15 ml conical tube containing neutralizing soluJon (10 ml HBSS+CM + 100 mg Trypsin inhibitor (Sigma) + 100 mg bovine serum albumin (Sigma)). This suspension was centrifuged once more for 8 minutes at 850 rpm, and the supernatant was removed. The remaining cells were resuspended in approximately 1 ml of 1X PBS containing 5 µl of molecular-grade BSA (New England Biolabs) and 2 µl of RNAse-inhibitor (Biosearch Technologies). Cell concentraJon in this suspension was assessed using a standard hemacytometer (a 50:50 sample of cell suspension + Trypan blue). The cell suspension was diluted with the same resuspension medium unJl the final concentraJon was appropriate for loading into the 10X Genomics Chromium chip.

Single-cell RNA-sequencing - data collec;on: Cell suspensions were introduced to 10X Genomics Chromium chips at concentraJons appropriate for retrieval of data from an esJmated 7,000 cells. Two Chromium wells were loaded per pooled biological replicate, with an anJcipated 14,000 cells retrieved per replicate. cDNA and libraries were constructed based on the 10X Genomics Chromium 3' gene-expression chemistry (v3.1). Library fragment size and variaJon were inspected on an Agilent 2100 bioanalyzer, then pooled for sequencing. Sequencing was done using the Novaseq 6000 playorm, with the aim of sequencing 40,000 reads per cell.

Published datasets were retrieved from GEO or ArrayExpress, per instrucJons from original publicaJons 50,52,72 .

Single-cell RNA-sequencing - data processing: Raw sequencing data was processed first with Cellranger mkfastq, and those outputs were then run in Cellranger count to produce count matrices. Reads were mapped onto the mus musculus GRCm38-mm10 genome reference. Post- hoc quality control per sample (including effecJve number of cells retrieved, number of reads and genes detected per cell, etc) are detailed in Table S1. Sample 16B (E16.5 cervical spinal cord replicate 1 reacJon 2) was excluded from further analyses at this stage based on an observably poor emulsion post Chromium run, poor read quality and almost negligible cell retrieval. Further quality control, clustering and annotaJon were done using Seurat v4 (though Seurat v5 was released in the interim) in R (v4.4.1). To begin in Seurat, data from each sample was imported into Seurat and minimum thresholds on count and gene numbers per cell, as well as maximum thresholds on mitochondrial gene percentages per cell were imposed (Table S1). From this, the data were log-normalized, variable features were found, and CCA integraJon (Seurat v4) was run using a list of biological replicates as inputs. At this stage, 195,146 cells were retained.

Principal component (PC) analysis (PCA) was run on the integrated dataset of all biological replicates using 90 PCs, while also regressing gene counts per cell. Ajer clustering all cells at resoluJon 2, clusters were annotated as major cell-types of the spinal column based on top marker genes per cluster (Table S2). Spinal cord neurons were extracted from this broader dataset of sensory neurons and non-neuronal cells, and this subset was further processed.

Principal component analysis of spinal cord neurons was once again done using 90 PCs, and the dataset was subsequently re-clustered, excluding clusters whose principal marker gene sets were non-neuronal, whose principal marker genes were a combinaJon of neuronal and nonneuronal in addiJon to substanJally higher gene counts (assumed doublets), and/or whose features were of a variety of cell-types in addiJon to substanJally lower gene counts (assumed unhealthy cells and/or data produced from poor emulsion quality).

Clustering of spinal cord neurons was done using 90 PCs at resoluJon 4. Finally, we split the dataset into three major groups: Group 1 - the well-described cardinal neuron classes as Group 1 (dI1-dI6, v0-v3 and motor neurons); Group 2 - the presumpJve dILA neurons; and Group 3 the presumpJve dILB neurons. As the dimensionality of this dataset was evidently quite high, dILA and dILB neurons were clustered together using 150 PCs and annotated by family at resoluJon 32, while cardinal neuron classes were clustered separately using 180 PCs and annotated by class at resoluJon 32. Neurons were annotated by cluster and also by major classes (families of dIL neurons or cardinal classes) and these annotaJons were re-introduced to the dataset comprising all spinal cord neurons. This version of the dataset was presented in main figures, and used as a reference to annotate spaJal transcriptomics data as well as to annotate single-nucleus RNA-sequencing data retrieved from Qiu and colleagues (described below) 72 . At this final stage of processing, 94,048 spinal cord neurons were retained.

PHATE trajectory inference was performed using PHATE 71 (R library 'phateR'), with the following parameters: knn = 20, gamma = 0, t = 100, and 90 PCs for dimensionality reducJon and visualizaJon. Cluster robustness was performed for all neurons at the level of family, as well as the refined types of dILA and dILB using Silhoue'e and LISI scores (R libraries 'lisi' and 'cluster') and computed with 90 principal components (PCs) each. Dendrograms for different neuronal subsets were generated based on centroid distances. The primary dendrogram, including all refined neuron types, was constructed using 150 PCs (Figure 1F). A second, familylevel dendrogram-excluding "Hindbrain"-like, "Immature", and "Other" neuron types-was generated using 90 PCs (Figure S3). AddiJonally, dendrograms for the dILA and dILA separately (Figure S3) were constructed using 90 PCs.

Single-cell RNA-sequencing - annota;on transfer to published datasets: Data from Qiu and colleagues were retrieved in order to examine how dIL families arise at precisely defined embryonic stages. We first extracted all CNS neurons from Jmepoints E11.0 to E16.75 and, using iteraJve clustering and filtering, excluded all clusters which were evidently not spinal neurons - exclusion criteria included presence of Hox genes of group 1-3 but not group 4 and beyond, Lhx6/Lhx8 (presumpJve forebrain inhibitory), Tfap2d (presumpJve midbrain) and excitatory En1+ cells (presumpJve midbrain). Ajer first-pass filtering, data from all Jmepoints between E11.0 to E16.75 were integrated together using Seurat v5 integraJon, reclustered using 120 PCs, and cell-type idenJty was predicted by transferring annotaJons from single-cell RNA-sequencing data generated here (see Single-cell RNA-sequencing - data processing and Fig. 1) using 120 PCs. As post-integraJon clustering allowed clear separaJon of hindbrain and nonhindbrain cardinal classes by absence or presence of Hox group 4 genes and beyond, hindbrain clusters were further filtered using these criteria. From this point, a conJngency table of predicted cell types was generated for each individual Jme point. Data were presented as the fracJon of all dIL families and immature neurons present at each specific Jmepoint (done separately for dILA and dILB neurons).

Single-cell RNA-sequencing - examina;on of published data: To examine onset of Zic gene expression in dIL neurons, single-cell RNA-sequencing data from Delile et al. (2019) consisJng of only spinal cord neurons at Jmepoints E9.5, E10.5, E11.5, E12.5, and E13.5, as well as spinal cord neurons from Osseward and colleagues at E12.5 were integrated together (six input datasets. Cell numbers: (N=356 E9.5, N=1724 E10.5, N=3588 E11.5, N=4561 E12.5, N=5542 E13.5) 52 ; (N=2914 E12.5).) using Seurat v4 integraJon, and clustered using 60 PCs at resoluJon 32. Many annotaJons from Delile et al. (2019) (e.g. dI5.3, dI5.1) corresponded to immature dIL neurons which bore no markers corresponding to mature neuron cognates in single-cell/nucleus RNA-sequencing data generated here or from adult datasets. Accordingly, we annotated clusters based on markers present in dIL neurons which no longer bore immature markers (i.e. Robo3, Nhlh1/2, Klhl35); from data prior to and including E13.5, only dILB1 (Plscr5/Skor1/Tac1), dILB2 (Maf/Cbln1), dILA1 (Stc1/Plch1) and dILA2 (Rorb/Sall3) could be reliably idenJfied; dI5 was inferred to be clusters bearing Phox2a/Lmx1b, and dI4 was inferred to be early-born (Zsx3high+) Lbx1+ inhibitory neurons clustering separately from those expressing Dmrt3 (dI6). To examine ranked expression of Zic1 among pdL progenitors, neural progenitor-annotated data was retrieved from Delile et al. (2019) from Jmepoints E11.5 and E12.5 (both Jmepoints of which are confidently during dIL neurogenesis and beyond dI4/dI5 neurogenesis); to enrich pdL progenitors, ventral progenitors ( Dbx2 , Nkx6-1 , Nkx2-2 , and Olig2 ) and dI1-3 progenitors ( Olig3 )

were excluded by their respecJve marker sets. Remaining pdL progenitors expressed Gsx1 , Gsx2 and Ascl1 , as expected.

Spa;al transcriptomics - data collec;on: All Jssues used for spaJal transcriptomics were immersion fixed overnight during (20-24 h), idenJcally to those used for ISH (see Genera;on of embryonic spinal cord ;ssue ) and generally using adjacent secJons from the same embryos examined in ISH. To determine spaJal arrangement of dorsal horn neuron subtypes in normal and mutant spinal cords we used the 10X Genomics Xenium (v1) playorm. As much of the mutant Jssue analyzed here was fixed-frozen, and as Xenium v1 did not yet have a supported fixed frozen Jssue protocol, we followed the guide for fresh-frozen Jssues with modificaJons detailed below. CryosecJoning was done as described in CG000579 revision C (10X Genomics) and fixaJon and permeabilizaJon was done as described in CG000581 revision C (10X Genomics), except that the 30-minute incubaJon in fixaJon soluJon was omi'ed. Tissue chemistry was done as described in CG000582 revision F (10X Genomics). Probe-sets used were the v1 mouse brain panel (248 genes) plus 100 custom probes (Table S8) marking either classes or individual clusters from the single-cell RNA-sequencing dataset collected here. A reference dataset was produced using four wildtype E16.5 spinal cords spanning upper cervical to sacral segments, to most accurately capture the variaJon of the dorsal horn across segments and animals, and these comprised the first of two slides. The second slide sought to capture a variety of mutant Jssues. OperaJon of the Xenium Analyzer instrument was done as described in CG000584 revision E (10X Genomics). SecJons were grouped by animal or genotype during Xenium analyzer setup, and these data are also noted in the table. Areas which contained overlapping secJons were omi'ed from this step and were not further analyzed.

Spa;al transcriptomics - data processing: Raw 10X Xenium datasets obtained from the Xenium analyzer computer were imported into Seurat, cells with 0 transcripts were removed, all cells in a group were clustered using 60 PCs at 0.5 resoluJon, and Seurat objects were wri'en as .RDS files (see code Xenium_2024_1_FilterClusterExport on Github). Subsequently, coordinates were assigned bounding each secJon, clusters and gene markers were displayed on a UMAP reducJon, and non-neuronal and sensory neuron clusters were excluded from further processing (see code Xenium_2024_2_DefineROI_ExtractNeurons on Github). From filtered spinal neurons, annotaJons were predicted using Robust Cell-Type DecomposiJon (RCTD) 50,78 from the R package spacexr and our single-cell RNA-sequencing annotaJons (described above) as reference. Based on predicted cell-types, family-level metadata was assigned to each cell. Further, a secJon idenJfier, and an esJmated spinal segment were added as metadata. AddiJonal exclusion of non-neuronal clusters was done here, parJcularly for clusters which had widely indisJnct or sparse annotaJon transfer; on inspecJon, these were ojen clusters with most cells bearing both neuronal and non-neuronal markers resulJng from poor cell segmentaJon or minor cell overlay. Cell-type proporJons were also examined here separately per spinal cord zone (C1-C4 - Cervical, C5-C8 - Brachial, Thoracic, L1-L3 - Upper lumbar, L4-L6 Lower lumbar or Sacral); to ensure proporJons were not affected by exclusion of parJcular parts of a secJon only complete secJons or complete hemisecJons were included; from these, cell proporJons are represented as a fracJon of all neurons from the total secJons or hemisecJons examined of a single embryo. Finally, cell coordinates and appropriate metadata were exported as .csv files for localizaJon analysis (see code Xenium_2024_3_RCTD on Github for above processing steps). Subsequently, anatomical landmarks to be used as anchors for coordinate normalizaJon were idenJfied for each individual secJon retained as this stage (see EdU counts - coordinate rota;on and normaliza;on for details). Coordinates from all secJons of a given genotype and/or spinal segment were combined and overlaid on each other to best visualize neuron-type locaJon across secJons. These coordinates were subsequently exported as sca'er plots for figures (see code Xenium_2024_4_Coordinate_processing_overlay on Github for above processing steps). To illustrate the relaJve dorso-ventral frequency of neurons from each dIL family (Figure 3C), the normalized y-coordinate of all dIL neurons was assigned to one of 200 bins spanning the full extent of both dorsal and ventral horns. To understand changes in cell-type frequency between genotypes, conJgency tables of predicted cell-types from E16.5 wild-type, Zic1/4 -null and Zic3 -null spinal cords were generated separately for each biological replicate and then normalized to the number secJons collected per replicate. Selected cell-type frequencies were compared across all three groups in a twoway ANOVA (cell-type x genotype), and the post-hoc Tukey's mulJple comparisons test was done between all genotypes for each cell-type.

metadata.preprint_title: Ontogeny of the spinal cord dorsal horn

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Ontogeny of the spinal cord dorsal horn
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Roome, R. B.; Yadav, A.; Flores, L.; Puarr, A.; Nardini, D.; Richardson, A.; Waclaw, R. R.; Arkell, R. M.; Menon, V.; Johnson, J. E.; Levine, A.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract:
The dorsal horn of the mammalian spinal cord is an exquisite example of form serving function. It is comprised of diverse neuronal populations stacked into laminae, each of which receives different circuit connections and plays specialized roles in behavior. An outstanding question is how this organization emerges during development from an apparently homogeneous pool of neural progenitors. Here, we found that dorsal neurons are diversified by time, with families of related cell types born as temporal cohorts, and by a spatial-molecular gradient that specifies the full array of individual cell types. Excitatory dorsal neurons then settle in a chronotopic arrangement that transforms their progressive birthdates into anatomical order. This establishes the dorsal horn laminae, as these neurons are also required for spatial organization of inhibitory neurons and sensory axons. This work reveals essential ontogenetic principles that shape dorsal progenitors into the diverse cell types and architecture that subserve sensorimotor behavior.

Highlights- Temporal cohorts of late-born dorsal neurons give rise to neuronal families
- Sequentially-born excitatory neuron families form adjacent laminae
- Laminar structure specifically requires excitatory (but not inhibitory or sensory) neurons
- Graded expression of Zic transcription factors directs fine neuronal identity
